region_of_origin: Tropical regions; widely consumed in Singapore

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of pineapple juice is approximately 250 ml (one glass), equivalent to 250 g, commonly consumed as a breakfast beverage or refreshment in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pineapple Juice — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for pineapple juice (unsweetened, 100% fruit juice). All per-100g values match the input record which carries HPB provenance at 0.95 confidence. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and select micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pineapple juice (unsweetened) GI = 46 (low) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (46 × 30.5) / 100 = 14.03 ≈ 14.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.6 g/100 g) in liquid form; low sodium (4 mg/100 g) is favourable but does not offset the sugar-driven cardiovascular risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit pineapple juice to no more than 100–125 ml per occasion and prefer whole pineapple to benefit from fibre-mediated sugar modulation; the AHA (2018) identifies sugar-sweetened beverages as an independent risk factor for elevated blood pressure regardless of sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 10.6 g sugar/100 g and only 0.2 g dietary fibre/100 g; liquid fructose-glucose mixture absorbs rapidly causing significant postprandial glycaemic spike
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pineapple juice should be avoided or strictly limited (≤100 ml) by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the absence of fibre means there is no modulation of glucose absorption, and the beverage triggers a rapid glycaemic response — whole pineapple in small portions is a safer alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar content (10.6 g/100 g) in beverage form contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and hepatic de novo lipogenesis; potassium (150 mg/100 g) is cardioprotective but insufficient to offset sugar risk at typical serving volumes
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict pineapple juice to occasional small servings (≤125 ml) and prioritise whole fruit; the high liquid sugar load is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines, while the potassium content offers modest vascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High fructose load (10.6 g sugar/100 g) without the bromelain-fibre matrix of whole pineapple; concentrated sugar promotes pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer whole pineapple over juice to retain bromelain (an anti-inflammatory enzyme) and fibre; pineapple juice provides concentrated sugar without the anti-inflammatory benefits of the intact fruit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pineapple juice is a sweet, tropical beverage produced by pressing or mechanically extracting juice from ripe pineapple (Ananas comosus) fruit. It is widely consumed across Singapore and Southeast Asia as a refreshing drink at any meal occasion, and is available both freshly pressed and commercially packaged. The juice has a bright golden-yellow colour, a distinctly sweet and mildly tart flavour profile, and a thin, watery texture with no pulp in filtered versions. Per 100 g, pineapple juice provides approximately 51 kcal, 12.2 g carbohydrate (of which 10.6 g is sugar), 0.41 g protein, 0.08 g fat, and only 0.2 g dietary fibre. It contains modest amounts of potassium (150 mg), calcium (8 mg), magnesium (10 mg), iron (0.18 mg), and vitamin C (~10 mg). The glycemic index of unsweetened pineapple juice is approximately 46 (low GI), but the glycemic load per standard 250 ml serving is approximately 14, placing it in the medium-to-high GL range. Clinically, pineapple juice is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its high free-sugar content relative to its negligible fibre content. This makes it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without strict portion control. Unlike whole pineapple, the juice lacks the bromelain-rich fibre matrix that moderates sugar absorption and provides anti-inflammatory benefit. It is naturally vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is pineapple juice good for diabetes?
Can I drink pineapple juice if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a glass of pineapple juice?
Is pineapple juice high in sugar?
What is the GI of pineapple juice?
Pineapple juice vs whole pineapple — which is better?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with 0.95 confidence in the input record. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008). All mandatory fields are complete and supported by approved primary sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for pineapple juice are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry used as the primary source. Saturated fat is estimated at 0.01 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 167771, Pineapple juice, canned or bottled, unsweetened) as a cross-reference. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g — confirmed zero as pineapple juice is a plant-derived beverage containing no animal-derived lipids. Vitamin C is reported at approximately 10 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central cross-reference. The input record classified the primary cuisine as 'Indian', which has been revised to 'Global / Singaporean' as pineapple juice is a universally consumed beverage with strong regional presence in Singapore; the Indian classification in the input is not supported by the food's cultural profile. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per G6 rules.
